zotero.abstractNote: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize objects from classes that were not seen during training. This is typically achieved by mapping visual features to a semantic space, such as attributes or word embeddings. In this paper, we propose using DNA sequences as a novel source of side information for fine-grained ZSL. DNA sequences provide a rich, objective, and evolutionary-based representation of species, which we hypothesize can better capture the fine-grained relationships between classes compared to human-annotated attributes. We propose a framework that learns a mapping from visual features to a DNA-based semantic space. We evaluate our approach on the CUB-200-2011 dataset and show that it achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods that use attributes or word embeddings. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our DNA-based approach is more robust to the lack of human-annotated attributes, making it a promising direction for fine-grained ZSL in scenarios where such annotations are difficult or expensive to obtain.
zotero.conferenceName: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (NeurIPS 2021)

extra.Editors: M. Ranzato and A. Beygelzimer and Y. Dauphin and P.S. Liang and J. Wortman Vaughan
